'''Christopher John Ashton''' (born 29 March 1987)<ref>{{citation|url=http://www.rfu.com/SquadsAndPlayers/EnglandElite/ChrisAshton.aspx|title=Chris Ashton|publisher=Rugby Football Union|access-date=24 February 2011|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110810012702/http://www.rfu.com/SquadsAndPlayers/EnglandElite/ChrisAshton.aspx|archive-date=10 August 2011}}</ref> is a retired English [[rugby union]] and former [[rugby league]] footballer, [[List of dual-code rugby internationals#England|one of the few players]] who have represented England in both rugby codes.  Ashton primarily played [[Wing (rugby union)|wing]] and secondarily played [[Fullback (rugby union)|fullback]].

Ashton first played as a {{rlp|fb}} or {{rlp|wg}} for [[Wigan Warriors]] in rugby league's [[Super League]] competition and won four caps for [[England national rugby league team|England]] in 2006 and 2007.

In 2007 Ashton signed for [[Northampton Saints]] in rugby union's [[Premiership Rugby|Premiership]], before moving to [[Saracens F.C.|Saracens]] in 2012, [[RC Toulonnais|Toulon]] in 2017, Sale in 2018 and Harlequins in 2020; he finished his career at [[Leicester Tigers]] in 2023. He made his debut for [[England national rugby union team|England]] in March 2010 and played regularly until 2014, including in the [[2011 Rugby World Cup]] where he finished as the tournament's joint leading try-scorer. After a break of four years, he was recalled to the England squad in November 2018. In April 2022 he became the record try scorer in [[Premiership Rugby]] after scoring a hat trick against [[Bristol Bears|Bristol]] to take him to 95 tries, in April 2023 he became the first player to 100 tries after another hat trick against [[Exeter Chiefs]]. In all Ashton won 40 caps for England.

==Rugby league career==
===Wigan Warriors===
Ashton's position at [[Wigan Warriors]] was normally {{rlp|fb}}, although he also played on the {{rlp|wg}}.<ref name="Statistics at rugbyleagueproject.org"/> He came through Wigan's scholarship programme and played for Wigan's U18s in 2003. He made his senior debut in Wigan's last match of the 2005 season, and scored two of their seven tries in a 36–22 victory over [[Huddersfield Giants]].<ref>{{cite news|url=http://menmedia.co.uk/manchestereveningnews/sport/rugby_league/s/174/174511_huddersfield_22_wigan_36.html|title=Huddersfield 22 Wigan 36|newspaper=Manchester Evening News|date=18 September 2005|access-date=14 February 2011|archive-date=12 November 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121112172423/http://menmedia.co.uk/manchestereveningnews/sport/rugby_league/s/174/174511_huddersfield_22_wigan_36.html|url-status=dead}}</ref>

Ashton was brought into the Wigan squad at the start of 2006 as replacement for the injured [[Kris Radlinski]]. He was one of the most consistent players for Wigan during their disappointing [[Super League XI]] season and was a contender for the Young Player of the Year award. He finished the 2006 season with 15 tries from 29 appearances making him the leading try scorer for Wigan. Ashton was given the number 1 shirt, previously worn by Radlinski, at the start of 2007 despite the arrival of former [[Bradford Bulls]] full back [[Michael Withers]].

Ashton made 52 appearances in total for the Cherry and Whites, scoring 28 tries.

===International===
Ashton was selected for the 2004 Academy Origin Series and the 2004 victorious England Academy U-18s tour to Australia.

Although some criticisms were made about his defensive abilities, he earned a call into the England squad at the end of 2006 while Great Britain were on tour in Australia and New Zealand.<ref>{{cite web |title=Rhino quintet handed England call |url=https://newsimg.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/international_and_australian/5282740.stm |website=BBC Sport |access-date=11 January 2026 |date=24 August 2006}}</ref> Ashton made his [[England national rugby league team|England]] debut against [[France national rugby league team|France]] on 22 October 2006 in the [[Federation Shield]] tournament. He played in all four Federation Shield matches, which concluded with England winning the competition, and he scored three tries in his four games.

==Rugby union career==
===Club career===
====Northampton Saints====

On 19 March 2007 it was announced that Ashton had signed to play [[rugby union]] for [[Northampton Saints]], after [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] granted him an early release from his contract.<ref name="Ashton handed early Wigan release">{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/super_league/wigan/6954800.stm|title=Ashton handed early Wigan release|work=BBC Sport|date=20 August 2007|access-date=20 August 2007}}</ref>

On 1 September 2007, Ashton made his debut for Northampton at [[Franklin's Gardens]] as a late substitute for [[Will Harries]] against [[London Welsh RFC|London Welsh]] in [[National Division One]]. He scored his first try for the club, with his first touch of the ball, in the same game which Saints won 44–11.<ref name="Six-try Saints secure full points">{{cite web|url=http://www.northamptonsaints.co.uk/32_4663.php|title=Six-try Saints secure full points|publisher=Northampton Saints|date=1 September 2007|access-date=1 September 2007|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070928121747/http://www.northamptonsaints.co.uk/32_4663.php|archive-date=28 September 2007|url-status=dead}}</ref>

The following week he scored for Northampton's second team, Northampton Wanderers.<ref name="Budgen scores on comeback at Castle Park">{{cite web|url=http://www.northamptonsaints.co.uk/32_4685.php|title=Budgen scores on comeback at Castle Park|publisher=Northampton Saints|date=10 September 2007|access-date=10 September 2007|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070922151726/http://www.northamptonsaints.co.uk/32_4685.php|archive-date=22 September 2007|url-status=dead}}</ref> and a substitute scoring appearance against [[Moseley]].<ref name="10-try Saints put Moseley to the sword">{{cite web|url=http://www.northamptonsaints.co.uk/32_4694.php|title=10-try Saints put Moseley to the sword|publisher=Northampton Saints|date=15 September 2007|access-date=15 September 2007|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081205045321/http://www.northamptonsaints.co.uk/32_4694.php|archive-date=5 December 2008 }}</ref>

In 2008, Ashton broke the [[National Division One]] try scoring record, scoring 39 tries (and one conversion) from only 25 appearances.<ref name="Chris Ashton Profile">{{cite web|url=http://www.northamptonsaints.co.uk/27_3900.php|title=Chris Ashton profile|publisher=Northampton Saints|date=24 April 2008|access-date=28 April 2008|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080327014344/http://www.northamptonsaints.co.uk/27_3900.php|archive-date=27 March 2008|url-status=dead}}</ref>

Despite a relatively disappointing 2008–09 season, with many appearances in the Wanderers and struggling to make it into the first team regularly, in the 2009–10 season Ashton embarked on a run of form that brought 15 tries in 16 games in all competitions including Heineken Cup efforts against [[Munster Rugby|Munster]] and [[USA Perpignan|Perpignan]].

Ashton was named as the Premiership player of the season for 2009–10.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_union/english/8690707.stm|title=Chris Ashton named Premiership player of season|work=BBC Sport|date=18 May 2010|access-date=20 June 2010}}</ref>

On 19 January 2012, it was announced that Ashton had signed with Saracens, and joined up with his 
new team for the start of the 2012–13 season.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-union/16551828|title=Chris Ashton to join Saracens from Northampton Saints|work=BBC Sport|date=19 January 2012|access-date=20 January 2012}}</ref>

====Saracens====
[[File:Chris Ashton 4882 - crop.jpg|thumb|upright|Chris Ashton in January 2014]]
Ashton's Saracens contract was rumoured to be worth £250,000 per season. On 1 September 2012, he made his [[Saracens F.C.|Saracens]] debut against [[London Irish]]. He scored two tries on his début, in a game which Saracens won 40–3.

Saracens finished the [[2012–13 English Premiership (rugby union)|2012–13 regular season]] at the top of the table, 3 points ahead of [[Leicester Tigers]]. However, in the play-offs they lost their semi-final match against Northampton, Ashton's former club, by 13 points to 27. Ashton scored a total of 6 tries that season.

In the [[2013–14 English Premiership (rugby union)|2013–14 season]], Saracens again finished the regular season as league leaders, 9 points clear of Northampton. They then won their semi-final against Harlequins, including a try from Ashton in the 61st minute. However, in a hard-fought final at [[Twickenham Stadium|Twickenham]] they again lost to Northampton, by 20 points to 24. Ashton finished the season joint-4th in the try scoring table, with a total of 8 tries.

In the [[2014–15 English Premiership (rugby union)|2014–15 Premiership season]], Saracens qualified for the play-offs in 4th place, with the same number of points as [[Exeter Chiefs]] (68), but taking the 4th spot on account of losing one less game than Exeter. In the semi-final Saracens again came up against Northampton, with the added disadvantage of playing away at [[Franklin's Gardens]]. However Saracens produced a strong defensive performance, with fly-half [[Owen Farrell]] producing an impressive kicking display, which saw Saracens win 29 points to 24. Ashton was on the replacements' bench.

In the final at Twickenham, Saracens played [[Bath Rugby|Bath]], who had a strong set of backs, all of whom were England internationals, and were expected to be tough opponents. However, Bath's challenge faltered as Saracens established early control of the game and did not lose the lead, eventually winning 28–16. Ashton came on as a substitute in the 60th minute.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-union/32883783|title=Premiership final: Bath 16-28 Saracens |publisher=[[BBC]] |date=30 May 2015 |access-date=11 October 2020}}</ref>

Ashton's strong performances throughout the season meant he finished in joint-2nd place on the try scoring tables with 13 tries, the same as fellow Saracens winger [[Chris Wyles]], behind Exeter Chiefs forward [[Thomas Waldrom]]. Ashton's impressive form led to him being called back up to the England training squad for the [[2015 Rugby World Cup]], which was held in England throughout September–October, although he was not selected in the final 31-man squad for the tournament.

In the 2015-16 season Ashton helped Saracens to both the Premiership title and the [[European Rugby Champions Cup]], starting both finals.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-union/36390531|title=Premiership final: Saracens 28-20 Exeter Chiefs |publisher=[[BBC]] |date=28 May 2016 |access-date=11 October 2020}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-union/36267607|title=Saracens beat Racing 92 to win first European Champions Cup|publisher=[[BBC]] |date=14 May 2016 |access-date=11 October 2020}}</ref> In the 2016-17 season Ashton helped Saracens retain their [[European Rugby Champions Cup]] title, scoring a try in the final against Clermont.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-union/39857333|title=European Champions Cup: Saracens beat Clermont 28-17 to retain European title|publisher=[[BBC]] |date=13 May 2017 |access-date=11 October 2020}}</ref>

Ashton received a 10-week ban in 2016 for eye-gouging; an appeal against the ban was dismissed. In September 2016 Ashton received a 13-week ban for biting [[Northampton Saints|Saints']] [[Alex Waller]] in a premiership match on 17 September. He was free to play again on 19 December.

====Toulon====
In October 2016, Ashton signed for French club [[RC Toulonnais|Toulon]] in the [[Top 14]] from the 2017–18 season.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2016/oct/25/chris-ashton-leave-saracens-join-toulon|newspaper=The Guardian|title=Toulon to sign Saracens and England utility back Chris Ashton|date=25 October 2016|access-date=18 February 2017}}</ref> Ashton played a single season in the Top 14, scoring 24 tries in 23 appearances to break the competition's try-scoring record previously held by Napolioni Nalaga.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.eurosport.co.uk/rugby/top-14/2017-2018/standingperson.shtml |publisher=Eurosport|title=Player Statistics Top 14 2017/2018|access-date=8 February 2019}}</ref>

====Sale Sharks====
On 3 July 2018 it was confirmed that Ashton was to return to England when he signed for [[Sale Sharks]] for the [[2018–19 Premiership Rugby|2018–19 season]].<ref name="BBC Sport">{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-union/44704441 |title=Chris Ashton: England winger signs for Premiership club Sale after leaving Toulon |work=[[BBC Sport]] |access-date=3 July 2018}}</ref>

Ashton left the club by mutual consent on 2 March 2020.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-union/51703900|work=BBC Sport|title=Chris Ashton: Sale Sharks agree termination of ex-England wing's contract|date=2 March 2020}}</ref>

====Harlequins====
On 4 March 2020, after his contract was terminated by Sale Sharks, Ashton signed for [[Harlequin F.C.|Harlequins]].<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-union/51738056|work=BBC Sport|title=Chris Ashton: Harlequins sign former England winger after Sale Sharks exit|access-date=4 March 2020}}</ref> Head of rugby [[Paul Gustard]] described him as "without doubt one of the most competitive, professional and dedicated athletes" he had come across.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-union/51738056|title=Chris Ashton: Harlequins sign former England winger after Sale Sharks exit|date=2020-03-04|work=BBC Sport|access-date=2020-03-04|language=en-gb}}</ref>

====Worcester Warriors====
On 25 January 2021, Ashton was granted early release from Harlequins as he joins with Premiership rivals [[Worcester Warriors]] with immediate effect until the end of the 2021-22 season.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-union/55801763|publisher=BBC Sport|title=Chris Ashton: Worcester Warriors sign England winger from Harlequins|date=25 January 2021|accessdate=25 January 2021}}</ref> On 13 December 2021, Worcester Warriors confirmed that Ashton had left the club by mutual consent.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://warriors.co.uk/2021/12/13/chris-ashton-leaves-warriors/|publisher=Worcester Warriors|title=Chris Ashton leaves Warriors|date=13 December 2021|accessdate=27 January 2022}}</ref>

====Leicester Tigers====
Ashton joined Leicester in February 2022 and made his debut against former club Northampton Saints on 12 February 2022, he made a try saving tackle while on during Freddie Burns' first half [[Head Injury Assessment]], but was then sin binned in the 70th minute after replacing Guy Porter just 3 minutes earlier.  Ashton scored his first try for Leicester against [[London Irish]] on 12 March 2022, then on 27 March 2022 he scored two tries in Leicester's win against [[Exeter Chiefs]]. The second of these tied the [[Premiership Rugby]] try scoring record on 92.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.leicestermercury.co.uk/sport/rugby/match-reports/exeter-chiefs-leicester-tigers-report-6867776|work=Leicester Mercury|author1= Bobby Bridge|author2=Ethan Clamp|title=Chris Ashton equals Premiership all-time tryscoring record as Leicester Tigers defeat Exeter Chiefs|date=27 March 2022|access-date=27 March 2022}}</ref> On 30 April 2022, Ashton scored three tries against [[Bristol Bears]] to take the Premiership Rugby record outright with 95 tries.

Ashton started the [[2021-22 Premiership Rugby|2022 Premiership Rugby final]] as Tigers beat his former side [[Saracens F.C.|Saracens]] 15-12.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-union/61829701|title=Premiership final: Leicester Tigers 15-12 Saracens - Freddie Burns drop-goal clinches title|website=BBC Sport|date=18 June 2022|accessdate=20 June 2022}}</ref> On 16 April 2023 Ashton became the first player to score 100 tries in Premiership Rugby, scoring a hat trick against [[Exeter Chiefs]] he took his career total to 101.<ref name="try">{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2023/apr/16/leicester-exeter-premiership-rugby-union-match-report|work=The Guardian|date=16 April 2023|access-date=29 May 2023|title=Chris Ashton reaches 100-try milestone in Leicester's thrashing of Exeter|first1=Gerard|last1=Meager}}</ref> Earlier that week he had announced his retirement at the end of the season.<ref name="try"/>

===International career===
[[File:Chris Ashton 2011.jpg|thumb|upright|left|Ashton during 2011 RWC]]
[[England national rugby union team|England]] manager [[Martin Johnson (rugby union)|Martin Johnson]] called Ashton up to the senior squad for the [[2010 Six Nations Championship|2010 Six Nations]].

Ashton made his England debut against [[France national rugby union team|France]] on 20 March 2010 in Paris. He played left wing, the opposite side to his usual position with Northampton. He gave the scoring pass to fellow Saints player [[Fullback (rugby union)|fullback]] [[Ben Foden]], who was also making his first [[Six Nations Championship|Six Nations]] start.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_union/8564550.stm|title=England drop Jonny Wilkinson for Toby Flood|work=BBC Sport|date=17 March 2010|access-date=21 May 2010}}</ref>

Ashton was taken on the summer tour to {{nrut|Australia}},<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/rugbyunion/international/australia/7821671/Australia-v-England-Martin-Johnsons-side-have-a-chance-to-silence-the-doubters.html|title=Australia v England: Martin Johnson's side have a chance to silence the doubters|date=11 June 2010|newspaper=The Telegraph| first=Mick| last=Cleary}}</ref> and played in both Test matches. Although England lost the first Test,<ref>{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_union/8734071.stm|title=Australia 27–17 England|date=12 June 2010|work=BBC Sport}}</ref> they won the second 20–21.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_union/english/8748378.stm|title=Australia 20–21 England|date=19 June 2010|work=BBC Sport}}</ref> Ashton scored his first international try in this match. On 13 November 2010, England defeated Australia at Twickenham. Ashton was the hero of the match with two tries in a 35–18 victory, the second after a run from inside the England's [[rugby union gameplay#Playing field|22-metre line]].<ref>[https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2010/nov/13/england-australia-autumn-international1 Chris Ashton stars as England overwhelm Australia in dazzling style also try off the year 2010] The Observer, 13 November 2010</ref>

Ashton scored two tries in England's opening match in the [[2011 Six Nations Championship|2011 Six Nations]] against [[Wales national rugby union team|Wales]] at the [[Millennium Stadium]] on 4 February, with England winning the game 26–19.<ref>[https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_union/9386391.stm Wales 19–26 England] BBC Sport, 4 February 2011</ref> He followed this with four tries against [[Italy national rugby union team|Italy]] the following week, equalling the all-time Six Nations record with six tries in a season. This performance also meant he is the first person in the Six Nations to score four tries in a single match. His famous swallow dive celebration (dubbed the 'Ash Splash') was used to celebrate two of these tries.<ref>[https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_union/9393726.stm England 59–13 Italy] BBC Sport, 12 February 2011</ref>

Ashton represented England at the 2011 Rugby World Cup in New Zealand and finished as the tournament's joint leading try-scorer with six.

He started all five matches for England in the 2012 Six Nations, and in May 2012 scored a hat-trick of tries as England beat the Barbarians 57–26 in a non-cap fixture at Twickenham. Ashton went on the 2012 summer tour to South Africa in which he started all three Test matches.

Ashton started all five matches for England in the [[2013 Six Nations Championship]], scoring a try in the first match against Scotland. England took the championship down to the final game against Wales, however suffered a heavy 30–3 defeat at the [[Millennium Stadium]] as Wales took the title. Ashton later played in all three of England's matches in the [[2013 end-of-year rugby union internationals|2013 autumn internationals]], scoring a try in their win over Argentina.

Ashton lost his place in the England elite squad, not playing in any of their games in the [[2014 Six Nations Championship|2014 Six Nations]]. He did however play in England's mid-year games against the [[New Zealand national rugby union team|All Blacks]] in New Zealand, all three of which England lost. Again, Ashton's defensive capabilities were the subject of much discussion during this campaign and he did not play for England during the [[2014 end-of-year rugby union internationals|2014 autumn internationals]] or [[2015 Six Nations Championship|2015 Six Nations]].

However, during Saracens' ultimately winning campaign in the [[2014–15 English Premiership (rugby union)|2014–15 premiership season]], Ashton consistently performed at a high level and this promising form resulted in [[Stuart Lancaster (rugby union)|Stuart Lancaster]] recalling him back into the England camp, including him in his 50-man training squad for that year's [[2015 Rugby World Cup|2015 World Cup]], alongside fellow Saracens winger [[David Strettle]], who had not played for England since 2008. Ashton was the subject of much media focus during England's World Cup training programme. Ashton was dropped from the squad some 6 weeks before the tournament.<ref name="Rugby World Cup 2015: Chris Ashton among those dropped in latest England squad cull by Stuart Lancaster">{{cite news|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/rugby/rugby-union/international/rugby-world-cup-2015-chris-ashton-among-those-dropped-in-latest-england-squad-cull-by-stuart-10446029.html|title=Rugby World Cup 2015: Chris Ashton among those dropped in latest England squad cull by Stuart Lancaster|newspaper=The Independent|date=7 August 2015|access-date=7 August 2015}}</ref>

Following England's disappointing campaign at their home World Cup, failing to make it out of the pool stages as they lost to both Wales and [[Australia national rugby union team|Australia]], Stuart Lancaster stepped down as England head coach and was replaced by [[Eddie Jones (rugby union)|Eddie Jones]].<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/rugbyunion/international/england/12006979/Eddie-Jones-named-England-rugby-coach-live.html|title=Eddie Jones named new England coach and says 'If you want to play for England you must play in Premiership'|newspaper=The Telegraph|date=20 November 2015|access-date=20 November 2015}}</ref>

Jones began making some immediate changes to the England elite squad, including dropping several long-standing players and adding a number of new or returning players. This included Chris Ashton, who was named in the squad for the upcoming [[2016 Six Nations Championship]]. However, Ashton was subsequently given a 10-week ban by the [[Rugby Football Union|RFU]] for eye-gouging [[Luke Marshall]] during an [[European Rugby Champions Cup|ERCC]] match between Saracens and [[Ulster Rugby|Ulster]].<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/rugbyunion/international/england/12113890/Chris-Ashton-ban-is-spectacularly-harsh-and-massive-shame-for-England.html|title=Chris Ashton ban is spectacularly harsh and massive shame for England|newspaper=The Telegraph|date=21 January 2016|access-date=21 January 2016}}</ref> Although Ashton appealed the ban, this was rejected and he therefore missed the entire 2016 Six Nations tournament and several games for Saracens.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2016/feb/04/england-chris-ashton-miss-six-nations-10-week-ban-appeal-rejected|title=Chris Ashton to miss Six Nations after appeal against 10-week ban rejected|work=[[The Guardian]]|date=4 February 2016|access-date=24 August 2020}}</ref>

A subsequent 13-week ban for biting<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2016/sep/27/chris-ashton-hopes-england-return-13-week-ban-biting-saracens-rugby-union|title=Chris Ashton hopes for England return despite 13-week ban for biting|newspaper=The Guardian|date=27 September 2016}}</ref> also ruled Ashton ineligible for selection for the 2016 Autumn internationals.

Ashton's decision to join Toulon in October 2016 appeared to end his hopes of an England recall, as he would not be eligible for selection by playing outside England. However, he returned to England with Sale next year, notably after winning a game against England with the [[Barbarian F.C.|Barbarians]] at [[Twickenham Stadium|Twickenham]] in May 2018, scoring a hat trick in a performance that saw England conceding a record points total at Twickenham. This game might have brought Ashton much closer to coming back in [[Eddie Jones (rugby union)|Eddie Jones]]' squad.<ref>{{Cite web|date=2018-05-27|title=Chris Ashton hat-trick makes forceful point in Barbarians defeat of England|url=http://www.theguardian.com/sport/2018/may/27/england-barbarians-match-report-chris-ashton|access-date=2021-10-05|website=the Guardian|language=en}}</ref>

Later in 2018, after being banned for seven weeks for a tip tackle in August 2018, was selected in the England squad for the Autumn internationals. In November 2018, he made his first England appearance in four years, coming off the bench in their 12–11 win over South Africa, before being named in the starting fifteen against New Zealand.

==Career honours==

=== Northampton Saints ===
* '''[[European Challenge Cup]]''' Champion: '''[[2008–09 European Challenge Cup|2008–09]]'''
* '''[[Anglo-Welsh Cup]]''' Champion: '''[[2009–10 LV Cup|2009–10]]'''

''Up to dates as of 16 April 2023''<ref name="its rugby profile">{{cite web |title=Chris ASHTON, Wing |url=https://www.itsrugby.co.uk/players/chris-ashton-11677.html |website=ITS Rugby |access-date=16 April 2023}}</ref>

==Playing style==
Ashton's support play, which he learnt during his days as a [[rugby league]] footballer, has been praised and is often cited as the main reason that he is such a prolific try scorer.<ref>[https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/rugbyunion/international/sixnations/8320898/Mike-Catt-Chris-Ashton-indicative-of-the-confidence-flowing-through-this-young-England-side.html Mike Catt: Chris Ashton indicative of the confidence flowing through this young England side] The Telegraph, 12 February 2011</ref>
Another of Ashton's key attributes is his speed, which he demonstrated by scoring a 90-metre try against Australia in the autumn of 2010,<ref>[http://rugbydump.blogspot.com/2010/11/chris-ashtons-brilliant-try-against.html Chris Ashton's brilliant try against the Wallabies at Twickenham] RugbyDump.com, 18 November 2010</ref> when he outran [[Drew Mitchell]] and [[James O'Connor (rugby)|James O'Connor]], two of Australia's fastest wingers.

When Ashton scores, he often performs a 'swallow dive', which has been nicknamed the 'Ash splash'.<ref name="celebration">[https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_union/english/9401316.stm England wing Chris Ashton defends his try celebrations ] BBC Sport, 17 February 2011</ref> He has been praised and rebuked in equal measure for it, with critics concerned about the risk of dropping the ball in the act of scoring.<ref name="celebration"/>
